case_name: Reservoir water cyanide control for drinking water under strict compliance and chemical-minimization constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for cyanide-impacted reservoir water intended for drinking water, balancing strict effluent compliance against low energy use, minimized chemical addition, and manageable operations.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal utility draws from a reservoir periodically affected by runoff from an upstream metal-finishing industrial area, creating episodic cyanide contamination along with elevated ammonia. The utility must reliably meet a very conservative finished-water cyanide target for potable use, but the owner prefers solutions with low energy demand and reduced chemical consumption because power costs are high and the utility wants to limit byproduct formation and chemical deliveries. Conventional clarification and filtration infrastructure already exists, so the trade-off is whether to add a targeted oxidation barrier, rely more heavily on adsorption, or implement a membrane route with stronger removal but poorer alignment with the operating constraints.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve stable potable-water compliance with total cyanide consistently reduced to at or below 0.01 mg/L while maintaining acceptable overall treated-water quality.
secondary_goals: minimize chemical dose and byproduct formation
preserve operability during episodic contaminant spikes
limit difficult residuals or concentrate handling

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer conventional solids handling and avoid large volumes of hazardous concentrate or chemically aggressive waste streams
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment must handle seasonal reservoir quality variation and short-term cyanide spikes without fragile process control

core_unit_functions.existing_coagulation_flocculation_sedime: remove particulate matter and lower oxidant demand from reservoir water
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: provide solids polishing and protect downstream units
core_unit_functions.controlled_oxidation_with_free_chlorine: oxidize cyanide to less hazardous species under controlled conditions
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: reduce residual organics, improve taste and odor, and provide polishing for oxidant byproduct precursors

applicability_rationale: cyanide in a moderate concentration range can be addressed effectively by a targeted oxidation barrier when pH and contact time are properly controlled
placing oxidation after conventional solids removal reduces unnecessary chemical demand from turbidity and natural organic matter
this route uses familiar drinking-water unit operations and avoids the high energy burden of pressure membranes
a polishing GAC step helps offset the chemical-minimization concern by reducing residual oxidant effects and improving overall treated-water quality
the process is robust for episodic contamination events and can be intensified during spikes without permanently high operating burden
potential_risks: chlorine demand may rise during high-DOC or high-ammonia periods, increasing dose requirements
careful control is needed to avoid excessive disinfection byproduct formation
if cyanide speciation shifts or spikes are sharp, monitoring and feed-forward control become important

core_unit_functions.existing_coagulation_flocculation_sedime: reduce suspended solids and lower ozone scavenging from particulates
core_unit_functions.rapid_sand_filtration: protect ozone and BAC performance by polishing solids
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize cyanide and improve biodegradability of residual organics
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon: remove biodegradable oxidation products and support ammonia reduction through biofiltration activity

applicability_rationale: ozonation followed by biologically active carbon offers a lower-chemical alternative than sustained chlorine-heavy oxidation
the BAC step is attractive because the second contaminant is ammonia, which can be partially addressed biologically under suitable operation
this train provides strong water-quality polishing for a reservoir source with moderate DOC and color
it can satisfy strict cyanide control while reducing dependence on bulk chemical deliveries if the utility can manage the added process sophistication
potential_risks: higher energy demand than chlorine-based oxidation may conflict with utility preference
ozone system maintenance and safety requirements increase operator burden
bromate risk would need evaluation if bromide is present or increases seasonally
BAC performance can be sensitive during startup and after disinfectant upsets

core_unit_functions.existing_coagulation_flocculation_sedime: remove suspended matter and reduce fouling load
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: provide adsorption and buffering of organic peaks while improving finished-water quality
core_unit_functions.controlled_post_oxidation_with_free_chlo: serve as a final cyanide destruction and compliance assurance step

applicability_rationale: this hybrid route is plausible when the utility wants to minimize oxidation chemical demand by first lowering competing organic constituents
dual-bed GAC improves robustness relative to a single adsorber and can smooth reservoir water variability
the final oxidation step provides a compliance safety barrier for a strict cyanide limit
it balances chemical minimization and reliability but is less direct than oxidation-first treatment
potential_risks: GAC alone is not the primary reliable barrier for cyanide, so compliance still depends on the post-oxidation step
media replacement frequency may be significant in a moderate-DOC reservoir
if oxidation is delayed too far downstream, upset conditions could reduce treatment margin

core_unit_functions.enhanced_pH_control: shift cyanide speciation toward volatile hydrogen cyanide for removal
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_tower_aeration: remove volatile hydrogen cyanide from water phase
core_unit_functions.existing_coagulation_flocculation_sedime: treat reservoir particulates and support downstream filtration

applicability_rationale: air stripping is technically capable for volatile cyanide species and has relatively low direct oxidant use
the route may look attractive under chemical-minimization goals
however, it is poorly aligned with drinking-water operation because it requires aggressive pH manipulation and off-gas control
potential_risks: off-gas handling for hydrogen cyanide is a major safety concern
speciation-dependent performance makes compliance less robust during variable reservoir conditions
substantial pH adjustment chemicals and subsequent stabilization may undermine the chemical-minimization objective
this route is uncommon and difficult to justify for potable treatment compared with oxidation barriers

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_with_coagulation_and_media_: protect membranes from particulate and organic fouling
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad contaminant rejection including cyanide-related species and dissolved constituents
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for corrosion control and finished-water acceptability

applicability_rationale: RO is technically capable and offers a strong barrier for a wide range of dissolved contaminants
it may be considered if the utility expects future multi-contaminant expansion beyond cyanide and ammonia
despite technical capability, it ranks low because the case priorities strongly favor low energy use, simpler operation, and avoidance of difficult concentrate management
potential_risks: concentrate disposal creates a difficult residual stream containing rejected cyanide and other dissolved contaminants
membrane fouling potential is meaningful for reservoir water with moderate DOC and seasonal turbidity variation
higher operator skill and monitoring requirements conflict with the preferred low-to-medium complexity envelope

preferred_rank_pattern: targeted oxidation integrated with existing conventional treatment should rank near the top for strict cyanide compliance
an ozone_or_BAC route should appear as a plausible but not clearly dominant alternative because it reduces some chemical dependence but uses more energy and adds complexity
reverse_osmosis should not rank first under low-energy and chemical-minimization constraints despite technical capability
at least one technically capable option should rank lower because of safety, residuals, or operational misalignment

must_reflect: very strict finished-water cyanide limit drives need for a reliable destruction or rejection barrier
low-energy preference penalizes membrane and ozone-heavy options
chemical minimization should create tension with chlorine-based oxidation rather than eliminating it outright
dual-contaminant context should consider that ammonia can increase chlorine demand and influence process ranking

must_contain: why conventional pretreatment helps reduce oxidant demand in reservoir water
why ammonia complicates cyanide oxidation and disinfection strategy
why adsorption alone is not the primary compliance barrier for cyanide in this case
why membrane concentrate handling and energy use downgrade RO

should_mention: disinfection byproduct or bromate concerns depending on oxidant choice
need for robust control during episodic contamination spikes
